coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots functionalized Ce-ZnO nanofibers with enriched oxygen vacancy sites morphology to improve the efficiency of selective electrochemical detection of Hg (II)
coredata.dc:creator: Qi, Xingpu
Wang, Zhengyun
coredata.dc:description:
Herein, GQD functionalized Ce-ZnO hybrid nanofibers (GQD/Ce-ZnO NFs) towards selective Hg2+ ion detection through DPV technique was investigated. The hybrid NFs were synthesized via a facile electrospinning process and their physical properties were investigated through XRD, SEM-EDS, HR-TEM with SAED pattern, XPS, UV-DRS, and ESR techniques. Notably, the plane value, phase recognition, and purity of the GQD/Ce-ZnO NFs were examined through XRD analysis. The chemical states, chemical composition, and weight percentage of the elements were examined by the XPS and EDS analysis. Similarly, the width of the hybrid NFs and structural morphology were elucidated through SEM and HR-TEM analysis. The existence of oxygen vacancies ( v
o) was verified using UV-DRS and ESR techniques. The electrochemical characteristics of GQD/Ce-ZnO NFs-modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was investigated using diverse electrochemical methodologies like cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV). Remarkably, DPV studies depict that GQD/Ce-ZnO NFs modified GCE achieved high anodic peak current 3.48 × 10−4 A upon successive addition of Hg2+ (0.1–100 μM). The anodic peak current was directly proportional to the mercury ions concentration throughout a wide range of 0.1 × 10−6 to 100 × 10−6 M with a LOD of 267 nM in an optimized experimental condition. The GQD/Ce-ZnO NFs modified GCE showed remarkable reproducibility, with an RSD value of 1.81 %. Additionally, it maintained a strong current retention rate of 71.5 % over 12 days, indicating commendable long-term stability.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots functionalized Ce-ZnO nanofibers with enriched oxygen vacancy sites morphology to improve the efficiency of selective electrochemical detection of Hg (II)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Herein, GQD functionalized Ce-ZnO hybrid nanofibers (GQD/Ce-ZnO NFs) towards selective Hg2+ ion detection through DPV technique was investigated. The hybrid NFs were synthesized via a facile electrospinning process and their physical properties were investigated through XRD, SEM-EDS, HR-TEM with SAED pattern, XPS, UV-DRS, and ESR techniques. Notably, the plane value, phase recognition, and purity of the GQD/Ce-ZnO NFs were examined through XRD analysis. The chemical states, chemical composition, and weight percentage of the elements were examined by the XPS and EDS analysis. Similarly, the width of the hybrid NFs and structural morphology were elucidated through SEM and HR-TEM analysis. The existence of oxygen vacancies ( v
o) was verified using UV-DRS and ESR techniques. The electrochemical characteristics of GQD/Ce-ZnO NFs-modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was investigated using diverse electrochemical methodologies like cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV). Remarkably, DPV studies depict that GQD/Ce-ZnO NFs modified GCE achieved high anodic peak current 3.48 × 10−4 A upon successive addition of Hg2+ (0.1–100 μM). The anodic peak current was directly proportional to the mercury ions concentration throughout a wide range of 0.1 × 10−6 to 100 × 10−6 M with a LOD of 267 nM in an optimized experimental condition. The GQD/Ce-ZnO NFs modified GCE showed remarkable reproducibility, with an RSD value of 1.81 %. Additionally, it maintained a strong current retention rate of 71.5 % over 12 days, indicating commendable long-term stability.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The datasets used and analyzed during the current study are available from the corresponding author upon request.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal ion contamination is considered to be a serious threat to human civilization and the aquatic system [1]. Environmental contamination is largely caused by heavy metal ions like arsenic, mercury, lead and cadmium even in very low doses. Importantly, the US Environmental Protection Agency states that the acceptable level of Hg2+ in water is to be within 10 nM to prevent any potential health issues [2]. Importantly, when mercury (Hg2+) and its constituents contact human organs including the heart muscle, brain and liver, they become extremely poisonous [3,4]. Additionally, at excessive exposure, they readily contact with enzymes and proteins to cause a variety of illnesses like renal malfunction and cause problems in the central nervous system which leads to death [5]. The concentration of Hg2+ ions was also another important perspective to be noted that can cause serious problems to the nervous system and kidneys. The environment has therefore received huge attention in the measurement of mercury ions in pharmaceutical items, food, tap water and sewage water. Subsequently, Atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) are a few common analytical methods for identifying Hg2+ ions present in water samples [6–8]. Likewise, we can detect the varying concentration of Hg2+ and other heavy metal ions in a simple spectrofluorometric analyzer [9]. However, due to the expensive, complex, and time-consuming features of the above-stated equipment, spectrometric techniques can't be used as a suitable technique for the in-situ determination of Hg2+.

Hereby, with a focus on public health, there is a need to develop trustworthy, very sensitive, and user-friendly technologies for the detection of mercury present in the water and environment. Several voltammetric techniques can be used in electrochemical procedures for mercury detection, owing to their sensitivity, selectivity, and rapid detection limits [10–12]. In this regard, electrochemical sensors have been considered a successful method in determining the presence of Hg2+ ions in drinking water. Furthermore, minimizing unwanted signals from other interference heavy metals with identical redox properties is difficult since electrochemical procedures are often dependent on redox processes [13–15]. Unfortunately, majority of them have low sensitivity and are interfered by other heavy metal ions. Anodic stripping voltammetry (ASV) is among the crucial and adaptable electroanalytical techniques that have been widely applied for determining metal ions. Briefly, Anodic stripping voltammetry (ASV) is an electrochemical technique that is widely used for the detection and quantification of metal ions in solution. It has several advantages over other electrochemical techniques, including high sensitivity, selectivity, and low detection limits. ASV can be used for the determination of a wide range of metal ions, including Hg (II), which is a highly toxic metal ion that is commonly found in drinking water.

To successfully detect Hg (II) using ASV, there are several important factors to consider. The first postulate is that the mercury ions must be able to adhere to the working electrode surface reversibly. This is crucial for the stripping of adsorbed Hg (II) during anodic scanning, which results in a peak in the current vs. potential plot that correlates to the concentration of Hg (II) in the water, allowing for effective Hg (II) detection through ASV [16]. Likewise, ASV has certain benefits over other electrochemical methods in the detection of Hg (II), such as improved sensitivity and selectivity [17]. This is because ASV allows for the preconcentration of metal ions onto the electrode surface, which in turn enhances the signal-to-noise ratio [18,19]. Additionally, ASV provides lower detection limits compared to other techniques, such as cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV). This means that ASV can detect Hg (II) at lower concentrations than other known techniques [20,21]. Although there are electrochemical sensors depending on enzymes, such as WS2 nanosheets embedded T7 exonuclease sensor [22], DNAzyme-based biosensors [23], and Oligonucleotide probes/enzyme-amplified electrochemical sensors [24], for the detection of Hg2+ ions. However, these enzymes are generally not favored for practical applications due to their various disadvantages. These drawbacks encompass denaturation, instability, high expenses, challenges in acquiring pure enzyme forms, storage problems, and shorter detection time [25,26]. Hence researchers are employed to produce simple and low-cost modified electrodes for Hg-detection with high reproducibility, selectivity and sensitivity. For this purpose, electrodes that have been chemically modified are intensively utilized in the electrochemical analysis [27].

Several semiconducting nanomaterials, including Sb2O3 [28], ZnO [29], CuO [30], Fe2O3 [31], WO3 [32] and SiO2 [33], as well as their composites, have higher potential for electrochemical sensing applications because they have several adsorption sites, large number of specific surface area and great electron-transfer kinetics [34]. Various synthesis techniques, including hydrothermal [35], sputtering [36], co-precipitation [37], sol-gel [38], chemical vapor deposition [39], and electrodeposition [40], are employed to create catalyst materials. However, these methods are limited to generate catalyst materials in three-dimensional (3D) and two-dimensional (2D) forms, which can restrict the available active surface area of the catalyst [41]. To overcome this limitation, nanofibers with one-dimensional (1D) structures are produced through the electrospinning process, which has the potential to augment the active surface area of the catalyst. Furthermore, 1D nanostructures have a large surface area which makes them easier to diffuse ions from the electrolyte medium to the surface of solid materials [42]. For this concept, extremely homogeneous nanofibers with 1D nanostructure are made inexpensively and repeatedly using the electrospinning and annealing process [43]. For instance, stacked graphene NFs with Au NPs composite, electrospun carbon NFs, PANI NFs/SWCNT, and Ag-NFs/IL NCs, were synthesized through electrospinning methods and subsequently employed for electrochemical sensing purposes [44–47]. Specifically, ZnO NFs have gained significant attention as a promising semiconducting material for the detection of Hg2+ ions due to their fascinating properties.

In recent years, significant attempts have been undertaken to increase the sensitivity of ZnO NFs-related sensors by increasing the specific surface area of ZnO NFs. In addition, ZnO NFs have cheap production costs, biocompatibility, non-toxicity, high adsorption capacity and chemical stability, making them great candidate materials for sensor applications [48,49]. Typically, cellulose acetate, poly(vinyl acetate), polyacrylonitrile (PAN) and poly(vinyl pyrrolidone) serve as supports to increase the plasticity of the nanofibers [50]. Several methods are used to enhance the physicochemical characteristics of nanostructured ZnO NFs [51]. Chemical doping is currently thought to be a successful strategy for achieving high sensitivity and selectivity at lower doses. In addition, doping causes crystal defects increases the surface-to-volume ratio and starts charge carrier traps [52].

In view of this, rare earth metals like Cerium (Ce) have been employed in this work due to their distinct catalytic capabilities in redox processes and numerous oxidation states (Ce2+−4+) which can enhance the rate of electron transfer ability in doped nanofiber. Among other rare earth metals (La, Gd, Tb, Yb, and Eu), Ce was specifically selected for its unique characteristics including its redox capability, precise sensing capability, and strong binding affinity [53]. Moreover, Ce doped ZnO NFs consider to be a new approach to improve their physicochemical characteristics by modifying the energy level redistribution [54]. Chemical doping of Ce in ZnO has been demonstrated to be an effective method for improving the sensitivity and selectivity of Hg (II) detection, especially at lower concentrations. Cerium doping in ZnO causes crystal defects and enhances the material's surface-to-volume ratio, increasing active sites accessible for Hg (II) adsorption and detection. Furthermore, charge carrier traps are formed on the Ce-doped ZnO surface, increasing the sensitivity and selectivity of Hg (II) detection by limiting interference from other ions [55,56]. However, Ce-ZnO nanomaterial suffers from the agglomeration of electrode material on the electrode surface during the electrochemical detection process which can lower the active surface area and sensing efficiency of the electrode [57]. So, hybrid nanofibers having a high surface area and improved carbon material have been incorporated into this work to solve this drawback.

Carbon allotropes, including single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs), multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and carbon-based derivatives, have been predominantly employed for their exceptional properties such as high surface area, electrical conductivity, and optical characteristics in sensing applications [58,59]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials which possess unique optical and electrical properties due to their size and structure was utilized. GQDs have a high surface-to-volume ratio, which results in a large number of edge sites that can facilitate electron transfer and increase the active surface area of the electrode. Additionally, their unique electronic structure, quantum confinement, and edge effects make them highly sensitive towards the detection of heavy metal ions, including Hg (II). When blended with Ce-ZnO NFs, GQDs can help to prevent the agglomeration of electrode material on the electrode surface, thus maintaining the active surface area and sensing efficiency of the electrode. This combination of Ce-ZnO NFs and GQDs results in a highly sensitive and selective electrode material for Hg (II) detection in water resources [60–62].

So far, several research works on the electrochemical detection of Hg (II) have been published. For instance, Laure et al. [63], deposited Au NPs on the surface of the GCE via the SWASV technique and used them for the determination of Hg (II) in water. The electrode achieved 80 pM as the LOD value in a current range between 0.4 and 6 nM. Likewise, Bagheri et al. [64], synthesized an Ionic liquid functionalized graphene-modified electrode for evaluating thallium, mercury, and lead in water samples. The potential of IL/Gr/CPE electrodes to perform at extremely low concentrations and short deposition time eliminates the need to remove oxygen from solutions and may increase the resolution of nearby peaks. The results revealed that the electrode detected 0.386 μM of Hg2+ within a linear range of 1.25–200 μM. Subsequently, Yan et al. [65], developed a polypyrrole-CNS nanocomposite through dilute polymerization and ultrasonication techniques and fabricated it as a screen-printed electrode. The PPy-CNSs screen-printed electrode was utilized for the selective detection of Hg (II) and Pb (II). The electrode detected 0.0128 nM of Hg (II) presence in water within 5 × 10−3 to 35 × 10−3 M linear response range. Moreover, Yu-feng et al. [66], synthesized amino-functionalized microspheres of carbon for the sensitive and selective determination of heavy metal ions (Cd (II), Pb (II), Cu (II), and Hg (II)) in water. The synthesis protocol includes the polycondensation of glucose in a hydrothermal reactor followed by the amination of as-synthesized CNS. The detection of Hg (II) was done in a SWASV technique which depicted that in a linear range of 1–1.9 μM, the PPy-CNS detected 1.07 × 103 nM of Hg (II). Likewise, Daoli et al. [67], fabricated highly advanced electrochemical cell using carbon nanotubes that have a thread-like structure, along with polystyrene and copper atoms (polystyrene@CNT/Cu). This remarkable cell was able to detect heavy metal ions using an ASV technique. Specifically, the cell successfully detected Hg2+ ions within a range of 1.5 to 6.0 μM, with an impressive LOD value of 1.05 nM. Siong et al. [68], conducted an intriguing study where they presented a straightforward method to synthesize Au NPs functionalized with graphene quantum dots (GQD-Au NPs). In their approach, Au NPs were reduced using an appropriate amount of cysteamine and then combined with GQD. To detect Hg2+ ions utilizing an ASV technique, the GQD-Au NPs modified GCE was employed. The research findings demonstrated that the modified GCE successfully detected Hg2+ ions within a response range of 0.02–0.1 μM, and the corresponding LOD values were determined to be 0.02 nM. Furthermore, Minh-Phuong et al. [69], developed an approach involving the design of paper electrodes that incorporated PEG-SH/SePs/AuNPs for dual detection of nitrate (NO3 −) and mercury (Hg2+) ions using the DPASV technique. The paper electrodes created by their research team proved to be successful in detecting Hg2+ ions within a linear range spanning from 0.07 to 17.0 μM. By analyzing the obtained results, the researchers were able to calculate the LOD values, which indicated that the fabricated electrode had the capability to detect Hg2+ concentrations as low as 5.0 nM. Similarly, Dan et al. [70], successfully constructed an electrochemical aptasensor for the highly sensitive and specific detection of Hg2+ ions. The aptasensor relied on the thymine-Hg2+-thymine (T-Hg2+-T) structure and utilized thionine (Th) as an indicator signal. To facilitate immobilization, streptavidin-modified magnetic beads (Fe3O4-SA) were firmly attached to an MGCE. The aptasensor exhibited remarkable performance, detecting Hg2+ ions within a linear range of 1.0 to 200 nM. Impressively, the aptasensor was capable of detecting extremely low concentrations, with a LOD as low as 0.33 nM for Hg2+ ions. The mentioned studies have limitations due to its limited linear range values in detecting Hg2+ ions. Notably, this current research aims to introduce a novel approach by incorporating functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) into the Ce-ZnO nanofiber matrix. The objective is to enhance the presence of surface oxygen vacancies within the nanofiber structure, which in turn promotes the electrochemical detection of Hg2+ ions. The results obtained from this study demonstrate the superiority of the GQD/Ce-ZnO nanofibers in detecting Hg2+ ions, as evidenced by their lower LOD values and narrower linear range compared to the findings presented in the existing literature.

The primary objective of this study is to synthesize and characterize Ce-ZnO NFs that have been functionalized with GQD. Additionally, the electrochemical applications of these GQD-functionalized Ce-ZnO NFs are investigated. The synthesized NFs were examined using various analytical methods such as XRD, SEM with EDS, HR-TEM with SAED pattern, XPS, UV-DRS, and ESR techniques. Furthermore, the interference of different analytes including Cd2+, Pb2+, Bi3+, Ni2+, Sb2+, Zn2+, and Cu2+ was investigated. The stability, and reproducibility of the GQD/Ce-ZnO NFs modified GCE was also tested. The modified GCE exhibited excellent reproducibility, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) value of 1.81 %. Moreover, it maintained a high current retention rate of 71.5 % over a period of 12 days, demonstrating remarkable long-term stability. Consequently, this research article presents GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs, with an increase in oxygen functionalities ( v
o), higher electrochemically active surface area (ECSA), and strong binding properties towards Hg2+ ions. These characteristics make the hybrid NFs suitable for the detection of Hg2+ ions, with LOD low as 267 nm. To the best of our knowledge, this study represents the first report describing a simple and reliable DPV approach for the highly sensitive detection of Hg2+ using GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs.

Graphene oxide (GO) was prepared via the modified hummers method [71]. GQD was synthesized from the prepared graphene oxide by using a probe sonication process. In brief, 500 mg of NaOH was added to a 100 mL beaker which is filled with 40 mL of C6H10O3 (ethyl acetoacetate), followed by stirring for 0.5 h at 650 rpm. To the filtered suspension about 0.5 mg/mL of GO was added and ultra-sonicated inside the bath sonicator at ice-cooled condition for 30 min. Then, the dispersed GO suspension was exfoliated in a probe sonicator for 3 h, while performing sonication the temperature was carefully maintained at 15 °C. The unexfoliated large fragments were removed by injecting the suspension into a cellulose membrane (0.22 μm). The resulting GQD solution was dialyzed continuously for 5 days inside a dialysis tube, it is to be noted that during dialysis the deionized water (DIW) was changed every 12 h. Finally, the suspension was freeze-dried inside a lyophilizer to get the powder form of GQD.

The initial step in synthesizing electrospun GQD functionalized Ce-doped ZnO (GQD/Ce-ZnO) hybrid NFs involves preparing a homogenous phase precursor solution. Accordingly, Cerous nitrate hexahydrate (Ce(NO3)3.6H2O) of known weight were added to 20 mL of DMF, subsequently, stirred at 60 °C for 3 h. Likewise, a sufficient amount of Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2.6H2O), PAN (10 wt%), and GQD was added to another beaker comprising 15 mL of DIW and stirred until dissolution at continuous heating condition. The previously acquired 20 mL DMF comprising Ce-precursor was poured into 15 mL of DIW solution and mixed constantly for 1 h to get a homogenous solution for the electrospinning process. Apparently, 18 kV/cm electric field was applied for 4 h of electrospinning in-between the capillary stainless steel tip and aluminium foil (ground) to get free-standing nanofibers. During electrospinning the flow rate of the tip was adjusted to 30 μL/min, the drum rotation speed was fixed to 1500–1600 rpm, the syringe needle and aluminium foil-wrapped drum is kept at a distance of 10 cm. In order to acquire pure GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs and to decompose PAN, the collected nanofibers were carefully calcinated at 450 °C for 3 h without destroying the fibrous nature of the hybrid nanocomposite. Following similar criteria, individual nanofibers such as GQD and Ce-ZnO NFs were synthesized by combining with PAN polymer for comparative and optimization purposes. The diagrammatic representation of the synthesis of composite nanofiber via the electrospinning process was displayed in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs modified GCE
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs were drop cast on the glassy carbon electrode (3 mm dia.). In specific, the catalytic ink for drop casting was prepared by mixing 0.03 mg/μL of GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs with a mixture of solvents (ethylene glycol: Nafion: ethanol = 25: 15: 60) and bath sonicated for 0.5 h. Before drop casting, the GCE was cautiously polished with 0.03 and 0.05 μm sized Al2O3 powder, afterwards, the electrodes were consecutively sonicated in water for 2 min, then in ethanol and dried inside N2 atmosphere. Also, 15 μL of the sonicated suspension was drop cast upon the clear surface of the GCE and dried at 40 °C for 4 h. For the sake of comparison, the GQD, Ce-ZnO NF modified GCE was also fabricated using the same protocol.

The presence of metal oxide, crystalline size, structure, and phase was evaluated by a powder X-ray diffractometer (XRD) within 2θ values 10°-90° and the scan rate was fixed as 10° min−1 throughout scanning. The hybrid nanofibers morphology, diameter and thickness were calculated from the scanning electron microscopy (SEM with EDS) analysis using CARL ZEISS EVO 18 instrument. The sample preparation step involves a sputtering layer of Au/Pd (70/30) upon the surface of samples. The high-resolution electronic images of the synthesized hybrid NFs were evaluated through HR-TEM analysis (TECNAI 20 FEI model). The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) acquired on an Auger spectrometer-Microlab 350 with an Al-Kα source to validate the binding energies, atomic percentage, ionic compositions and states (Energy-20 kV; Power-200 W; Excitation Energy-1480 eV). The absorbance and the energy band gap of the nanofibers were evaluated from the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectrum (UV-DRS) results (UV-2450 spectrophotometer). The oxygen vacancies ( v
o) were elucidated from the signals obtained from the electron spin-resonance (ESR-JES FA200) spectroscopy.

All of the electrochemical assessments were carried out in Autolab PGSTAT- Metrohm electrochemical workstation in a three-electrode set-up. Where, the GCE modified with synthesized NFs was used as the working electrode, platinum rod as the auxiliary electrode and Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl) as the reference electrode. Before all the experiments the phosphate buffer bath was purged with N2 of high purity to eliminate the excessive presence of oxygen and maintained under normal temperature. For every study, the pH was assigned as 7 in order to check the catalyst material's potential activity towards Hg (II) ion detection in tap water and other industrial wastewater in the future.

Fig. 2 displayed the XRD spectrum of GQD, Ce-ZnO, and GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs with the stimulated reference pattern of ZnO. In general, the synthesized nanofibers purity, plane value and phase recognition were examined by X-ray diffraction analysis. In Fig. 2(b), the availability of a broad peak for the (002) lattice plane situated at 28.3° was indexed to the graphene layers disarranged stacking arrangement, further, the non-existence of (001) plane strongly confirms that the graphene oxide (GO) has been effectively converted to GQD [72]. The obtained spectral information (Fig. 2(a)) is the stimulated reference pattern of wurtzite-phased ZnO with a hexagonal structure (JCPDS No: 00–021-1272). Besides, Ce-ZnO NFs exemplified eight individual peaks congruent to the (201), (112), (103), (110), (102), (101), (002) and (100) miller indices of the aforementioned JCPDS file confirming the successful synthesis of nanofibers (Fig. 2(c)) [73,74]. The 2θ value of Ce-ZnO and GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs display a slight shift towards a higher angle of diffraction because while undergoing the doping process Ce ions enter into the interstitial position of the ZnO lattice leaving behind no distinct peaks of Ce-atom. Subsequently, a hump peak originated at 28.3° indexing to the (002) miller indices similar to that of GQD NFs, justifying GQDs incorporation into the fibrous matrix of GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs (Fig. 2(d)) [75,76]. The primary peak intensities of the GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs were lower than those of Ce-ZnO NFs, which strongly evidenced that the hybrid nanostructure was satisfactorily constructed.

The SEM analysis was carried out at two varying magnifications (2 & 1 μm) to analyze the fibrous width, diameter of the nanoparticles attached on the nanofibers surface and structural morphology of the synthesized GQD, Ce-ZnO and GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs (Fig. 3 ). Image-J application was utilized to measure the diameters of all nanofiber matrices [77,78]. Correspondingly, all the SEM images displayed thread-like fibrous matrices, these fibrous matrixes of beta-sheets are aligned themselves as a consequence of successful nanofiber formation. Fig. 3(a&b) micrograph displays well-oriented interconnected thread formation without generation of agglomerated beads, whereas, the GQD NFs width was evaluated to be 208.5 ± 10 nm [79,80]. Next, the Ce-ZnO NFs micrographs presented in Fig. 3(c&d) demonstrated tube-shaped fibers decorated with agglomerated structures resembling lotus buds and the average diameter was estimated to be 189.4 ± 15 nm [81]. The lotus buds appearance in Ce-ZnO NFs is attributable to the availability of Ce and ZnO in the nanofibers matrix [82]. Furthermore, the GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs (Fig. 3(e&f)) images also showed lotus buds-like structures present all over the nanofibers inner and outer surfaces, similar to that of Ce-ZnO NFs and the average nanofiber diameter was evaluated as 140.13 ± 5 nm. Currently, the average diameter of the nanofibers decreases with the addition of GQD and Ce-ZnO in the GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs which well agrees with previously reported literature findings [83]. It is noted that the presence of Ce-atom and zinc oxide causes a change in the surface tension, viscosity, and conductivity of the polymer solution resulting in the formation of nanofibers with a smaller diameter as well as with a large surface area. Furthermore, the use of nanoparticles can accelerate the cooling rate throughout electrospinning, resulting in a decreased GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs diameter [84,85].

The energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) plot revealed the availability of cerium, zinc, carbon, and oxygen in the GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs (Fig. S1(a&b)). The weight proportion of zinc was 23.56 %, carbon was 18.68 %, oxygen was 45.49 %, and cerium was 12.27 %, as shown in the inset of Fig. S1a. From the EDS finding we can identify the vast availability of oxygen atom (45.49 %) in the synthesized GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs. The presence of surface oxygen vacancy ( v
o) was proposed to promote the overall electrochemical behaviour via adsorption of Hg2+ more effectively. Finally, the results indicate that integrating GQD to the Ce-ZnO NFs enriched GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs matrix with more ( v
o) and decreased nanofibers diameter which could significantly improve the sensitivity towards Hg (II) ion detection in water via offering enlarged active surface area.

The energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) plot revealed the availability of cerium, zinc, carbon, and oxygen in the GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs (Fig. S1(a&b)). The weight proportion of zinc was 23.56 %, carbon was 18.68 %, oxygen was 45.49 %, and cerium was 12.27 %, as shown in the inset of Fig. S1a. From the EDS finding we can identify the vast availability of oxygen atom (45.49 %) in the synthesized GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs. The presence of surface oxygen vacancy ( v
o) was proposed to promote the overall electrochemical behaviour via adsorption of Hg2+ more effectively. Finally, the results indicate that integrating GQD to the Ce-ZnO NFs enriched GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs matrix with more ( v
o) and decreased nanofibers diameter which could significantly improve the sensitivity towards Hg (II) ion detection in water via offering enlarged active surface area.

The HR-TEM images accompanied by the SAED pattern for the synthesized GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs were displayed in Fig. 4 at different resolutions (500, 200, 20, 10 & 5 nm). Moreover, all the HR-TEM images illustrated the presence of multiple tube-shaped morphologies surrounded by tiny black dots (Fig. 4(a-e)). The presence of GQD, Ce, and ZnO components within the synthesized GQD/Ce-ZnO nanofibers (NFs) was confirmed, as indicated by the existence of these small dots. Apparently, the average NFs diameter was determined from Fig. 4(c) which displayed the average GQD/Ce-ZnO NFs diameter as 149.5 nm which matches with the SEM findings. The particle size distribution graph was affixed in the inset of Fig. 4(c). Notably, the d-spacing values assessed from Fig. 4(f) confirmed the presence of ZnO with the d-spacing value of 0.26 nm congruent to the (002) lattice fringes [86]. Moreover, the interplanar distance value of 0.31 nm is attributable to (111) lattice planes of the doped Ce-atom into the interstitial sites of the ZnO lattice [87–89]. Similarly, the presence of GQD is demonstrated by the available d-spacing value of 0.32 nm, which corresponds to the (002) lattice fringes caused by the disarranged stacking arrangement of the graphene layers [90]. The obtained d-spacing values of GQD/Ce-ZnO NFs exhibit a clear correlation with the XRD results of Ce-ZnO (JCPDS No: 00–021-1272) and the amorphous phase of GQD. Similarly, Fig. 4(f) illustrates the uneven distribution of oxygen vacancies ( v
o), which are indicated by yellow-coloured rings. Furthermore, the SAED patterns inserted in the inset of Fig. 4(f) confirm the existence of wurtzite-phased ZnO with a hexagonal structure. It is hypothesized that the synergistic effect of Ce, ZnO, and GQD present in the GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs highly promotes the adsorption and oxidation of Hg (II).

XPS analysis for the synthesized GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs was recorded to precisely justify the chemical composition and chemical state present in the hybrid nanofibers. The high-resolution core level spectra of C 1s, O 1s, Ce 3d, and Zn 2p are displayed in Fig. 5 revealing the presence of such elements inside the hybrid NFs, indicating that no elemental loss occurred throughout the calcination process. Especially, C 1s high-resolution spectrum exhibited four different peaks (Fig. 5(a)) with the binding energy of 284.42, 285.70, 287.22 and 289.56 eV attributed to the CC (sp2), CC (sp3), -C=O and -O=C-OH bond. The spectrum demonstrates that both sp2 and sp3 hybridized CC are present in the hybrid nanofibers. Moreover, the presence of oxygen functionality in GQD was established by the carbonyl and carboxylic groups presence [91]. Fig. 5(b) depicts the O 1s core level spectrum in which the energy state was split into two distinct peaks attributable to binding energies of 532.77 and 534.58 eV. The aroused peaks were ascribed to the formation of two various oxygen species with the C and Zn-atoms. Importantly, the lower binding energy corresponds to the CO functionalities in the GQD and higher binding energy affirms the existence of oxygen vacancies ( v
o) in the ZnO matrix which can enhance the electrochemical properties via functioning as charge trappers [92]. The Ce 3d core level spectra (Fig. 5(c)) show Ce 3d5/2 and Ce 3d3/2 spin-orbit peaks at 884.42, 899.45, and 903.67 eV, respectively. The presented bulk Ce 3d spectrum revealed that both Ce3+ and Ce4+ bonding states co-exist in the GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs matrix [93]. The core level spectra of the Zn 2p energy state (Fig. 5(d)) depict the measured core level binding energies Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 as 1020.95 and 1044.03 eV, respectively [94,95]. The energy difference (23.08 eV) between these two peaks demonstrates that Zn occurs largely in the Zn2+ chemical form justifying that it is bonded to oxygen (O2−) and exists as ZnO in the GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs. Considering this, it is hypothesized that the existence of v
o in GQD/Ce-ZnO NFs and this v
o could potentially enhance the electrochemical characteristics for determining Hg (II).

Apart from confirming the presence of v
o in the synthesized NFs, it is imperative to substantiate the enhanced v
o enrichment in the GQD/Ce-ZnO NFs compared to Ce-ZnO and GQD. Consequently, UV-DRS studies were undertaken to validate the escalated v
o presence in the NFs by examining the absorbance and binding energy values (Fig. 6(a&b)). The GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs demonstrate significantly higher absorbance within the 200–800 nm wavelength range compared to GQD and Ce-ZnO (Fig. 6(a)). This enhanced absorbance can be attributed to the elevated concentration of v
o resulting from the integration of GQD into the Ce-ZnO NFs. In order to determine the optical energy band gap of the synthesized NFs, the Tauc's relation ( αhv = C
hv ×
E g
)
1 2
was examined using the data presented in Fig. 6(b). In this relation, h represents the Planck constant, Eg denotes the energy band gap, α corresponds to the optical absorption coefficients, and C represents a constant. Furthermore, the GQD/Ce-ZnO NFs exhibit a bandgap energy value (Eg) of 2.7 eV, which is significantly lower compared to GQD (3.16 eV) and Ce-ZnO (3.01 eV). The high amount of extrinsic and intrinsic electrical conductivity can be responsible for the noticeably low energy band gap value reported in the GQD/Ce-ZnO NFs. The material's improved electronic transport capabilities are made possible by the narrow band gap [96]. Additionally, v
o in the NFs reduces the effects of polarisation which in-turn enables quick electron transfer kinetics [97]. These qualities are very helpful in electrochemical sensing techniques because they guarantee rapid electronic transport, enabling effective and quick Hg2+ ion detection.

The considerable presence of oxygen vacancies ( v
o) is robustly substantiated by the results of the ESR investigation conducted at room temperature. The ESR spectrum results obtained for the GQD, Ce-ZnO, and GQD/Ce-ZnO NFs were presented in Fig. 6(c). The increased abundance of oxygen vacancies ( v
o) could potentially contribute to enhancing the selectivity and sensitivity for the detection of Hg2+ ions. It is observed that the GQD/Ce-ZnO NFs exhibited a prominently strong ESR signal at g = 2.05, indicating the presence of trapped electrons in the oxygen vacancies ( v
o) [98,99]. Notably, the intensity of the ESR signal for the Ce-ZnO NFs was lower compared to that of the GQD/Ce-ZnO NFs, suggesting that the Ce-ZnO NFs lack a sufficient amount of oxygen vacancies ( v
o). Furthermore, the ESR signals observed for the GQD were linear, implying the absence of any oxygen vacancies ( v
o). Thus, v
o promotes the electron movement during electrochemical detection processes by facilitating an enhancement in electrical conductivity.

The electrocatalytic behaviour of the GQD, Ce-ZnO and GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs coated on the GCE surface was evaluated inside an electrochemical bath comprising 5 mM K4[Fe(CN)6] in PBS with pH 7. The CV-curve displayed redox peak pairs for the synthesized electrocatalyst material at 50 mVs−1 scan rate. Briefly, the CV profiles depicted in Fig. 7(a) illustrate that the fabricated GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs display increased peak current (Ipa = 164.06 μA) with redox couple than GQD (Ipa = 104.64 μA) and Ce-ZnO (Ipa = 125.58 μA) NFs. Moreover, all the nanofibers effectively initiate the redox couple reaction of Fe(CN6)3-/4- than bare GCE with slight shifts in their oxidation potential such as 0.32 V for GQD NFs, 0.31 V for Ce-ZnO NFs and 0.29 V for GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs. Fig. 7(b&c) demonstrates the CV-curve of the GCE coated with GQD/Ce-ZnO NFs at varying scan rates (10–100 mVs−1), wherein, the increased scan rate is proportional to the observed peak current, the peaks exemplified excellent reversibility and superior material stability [100,101]. Besides, the increased peak current (Ipa = 330 μA) at 100 mV/s is due to the accelerated electrochemical reaction of GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs which in turn also enriched the GQD/Ce-ZnO modified GCE electrode with precise sensitivity. The highest peak current is achieved as a consequence of the large specific surface area and surface-bound oxygen functionalities offered by GQD [102]. The intrinsic Ce-atom doped ZnO being in its multivalent state tends to improve electron mobility and facilitate better electrochemical activity [103,104]. Herein, the concentration of active materials on the surface of the GCE-modified with GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs has been evaluated from the current Vs potential (i Vs v) plot based on the Brown-Anson Eq. (7) as mentioned beneath:
(7)
Ip =
n 2
F 2
I ∗
AV
4 RT

In which,
I* = surface concentration (mol/cm2).
n = number of electrons transferred.
F = Faraday constant (96,485C/mol).
A = surface area of the modified GCE.
V = scan rate (50 mV/s).
R = gas constant (8.314 J/mol K).
T = absolute temperature (298 K).

Eventually, the estimated surface concentration value for GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs is 2.77 × 10−6 mol cm−2. The maximum surface concentration value of GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs is due to the decrement in particle size owing to the incorporation of Ce-atom at the interstitial site of the ZnO lattice [105], as evident from the XRD results.

Fig. 7(d) shows the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy for the GCE modified with synthesized nanofibers in a PBS bath (pH = 7.0) containing 5 mM of K4[Fe(CN)6]. The Nyquist plot displays a semicircle in a high-frequency range followed by a straight line at a relatively low frequency. Notably, the semicircle observed at high-frequency range corresponds to the electronic conductivity of electrolyte among the active material, and substrate, and the occurrence of straight light inclined in-between 30-90° in the low-frequency range is attributed to the diffusion of [Fe(CN)6]4-/3- [106–108]. An equivalent circuit has been stimulated and fitted for the obtained EIS pattern which is affixed in the inset of Fig. 7(d), where, Rs, Rct, Zw and Cdl relate to the electrolyte resistance of bulk solution, charge exchange resistance of the electron, Warburg resistance and double layer capacitance of interfacial phase. Subsequently, the faradic reactions fast kinetic was noted with low Rct values and the electrode reaction kinetics was denoted by calculating faradic impedance (Rct + Zw) [109,110]. Notable, the GCE modified with GQD, Ce-ZnO, and GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs exhibited low Rs values of 5.98, 5.75, and 4.83 Ω. The prominent electronic conductivity of GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs coated GCE is evidenced by the lower Rct value (60.2 Ω) compared to GQD (82.69 Ω) and Ce-ZnO (80.6 Ω) NFs coated GCE which was much lower than bare GCE (101.6 Ω). The diffusion of ferrocyanide ions between the electrolyte and electrocatalyst material was represented by a straight line at a low-frequency range. Moreover, the transfer of electrons or ions between the electrodes and the electrolyte is the process that controls the speed of the reaction, indicated as Warburg impedance (Zw) [111,112]. GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs coated GCE displayed a linear line inclined at an angle close to 90° as Zw, justifying a higher rate of electrolyte ions diffusion on the electrode surface than the other two electrocatalyst materials. On the whole, the interconnection of Ce-ZnO nanofibers with GQD improved the electronic conductivity by offering enhanced surface contact with electrolyte ions, surface oxygen vacancy and vast active sites necessary for the diffusion of ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behaviour of Hg2+ on the GCE-modified with GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs
ce:section.ce:para:
Firstly, the differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) behaviour of mercury (Hg2+) was determined using GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs modified glassy carbon electrodes at a scan rate of 50 mVs−1 in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) containing HgCl2 solution. Importantly, the potential window chosen for the effective detection of mercury was in-between 0 and 0.4 V. The optimized pH for detecting the Hg2+ ion was determined as pH 7 (Ipa = 3.89 × 10−6 A), while in all other pH (1−10) except for pH 7, the electrocatalyst material exemplified lower current as shown in Fig. 8(a). The hydrolysis of Hg2+ at higher pH levels (>7.0) resulted in a sharp decline in peak current and can lead to the destruction of peak form [113,114]. Similar to the previous example, measurements at pH levels lower than 7.0 result in decreased oxidation current because the GQD/Ce-ZnO fibrous matrix degrades in a highly acidic medium. As well as the appropriate potential window for Hg2+ oxidation was determined by evaluating the electrochemical Hg2+ detection at varying initial scanning potentials (−0.8, −0.6, −0.4, −0.2, 0 & 0.2 V) in the DPV technique. In which, 0–0.4 V displayed increased peak current (Ipa = 3.37 × 10−6 A) and fixed to be the optimized potential window for pre-deposition of Hg2+ (Fig. 8(b)). A peak evolved at 0.2 V attributed to the metallic mercury oxidation which is illustrated by the following Eqs. (8) & (9):
(8)
2
Hg
2 +
+ 4
e −
→ 2
Hg 0
(9)

Hg 0
→ 2
Hg
2 +
+ 4
e −

The Hg2+ ion sensor first gets adsorbed on the surface of GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs modified GCE followed by oxidation at the second step. When Hg2+ (0.1–100 μM) is successively added to the GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs modified GCE, the anodic peak current increases (Ipa = 3.48 × 10−4 A) in the DPV plot (Fig. 8(c)). The corresponding linear fit graph (R2 = 0.998) is shown in Fig. 8(d), evidencing GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs modified GCE's enhanced electrochemical activity. Finally, we hypothesized that Ce-atom availability in the interstitial position of the ZnO lattice and GQD in the GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs enhances the percentage availability of oxygen vacancy, allows ease adsorption of Hg (II) ions and greatly increase the sensitivity via supplying sufficient electrons for Hg (II) redox reactions.

The selectivity of the electrocatalyst nanofibers was studied by the addition of various heavy metal ions bearing oxidation potential near to Hg2+ which can be possibly seen in the water sample. For this purpose, the electrochemical bath was prepared by adding the list of metal ions including Cd2+, Pb2+, Bi3+, Ni2+, Sb2+, Zn2+ and Cu2+ with Hg2+ (25 μM) in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7). The interference studies were undergone at a wide potential window (−1.2 to 0.6 V) to ensure the oxidation potential of different interfering heavy metal ions. Fig. 9(a) depicts the CV profiles in the presence of the above-listed interference metal ions, where, Hg2+ achieves a high peak current (Ipa = 3.88 × 10−5 A) at 0.2 V followed by separation of seven clear peaks corresponding to the potential interferent metal ions (Cd2+, Pb2+, Bi3+, Ni2+, Sb2+, Zn2+ and Cu2+) [115,116]. Additionally, the interference bar diagram represents the increased current values of Hg2+ ions in the presence of other heavy metal ions implying enhanced detection (Fig. 9(b)). Importantly, the major interferent in the detection of mercury is copper ion because Cu2+ ion (−0.08 V) exhibits oxidation potential near Hg2+ (0.2 V) metal ions. Further, DPV measurements have been carried out in the presence of 25 μM of Cu2+ in 0.1 M PBS with a pH of 7.0 (Fig. 9(c)), in order to assess the detection accuracy of the GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs modified GCE towards Hg2+ (0.1 to 100 μM). Similarly, within a separation potential of 0.29 V two separate anodic peaks arise for Hg2+ and Cu2+ achieving an Ipa value of 1.31 × 10−4 and 2.02 × 10−5 A at 100 μM concentration, respectively. A very clear signal was seen even at 0.1 μM Hg2+ in the presence of 25 μM Cu2+ ions demonstrating that the detection of very low concentrations of mercury is still achievable in the presence of 25 times as many copper ions. Fig. 9(d) displays the linear fit plot corresponding to the eq. Y = 1.67c + 1.59, R2 = 0.994 with the calculated limit of detection (LOD) value of 267 nM within 0.1 × 10−6 to 100 × 10−6 M concentration. The selectivity of mercury ion detection is due to the affinity of Hg2+ ions with GQD present in the hybrid NFs. Further, the formulated following Eqs. (10)–(15) demonstrates the possible steps involved in the Hg (II) detection by the GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs.

Moreover, the enhanced efficacy is owing to the GQDs surface oxygen functional groups that operate as Hg2+ ion adsorption sites and facilitate charge transfer from the GQDs surface to the GCE substrate. Surface energy and the interactions between the GQDs and the substrate have a major role in determining the rate of nucleation [117,118]. Also, the surface oxygen vacancy ( v
o) generated by Ce-atom and zinc oxide function as an electron trapper resulting in excellent detection of Hg2+ ions. Due to their high surface oxygen content, ZnO and Ce have been found to possess superior adsorption behaviour towards Hg2+ [119]. The schematic representation of effective mercury ion detection by the GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs coated GCE electrode was displayed in Fig. 10 . Furthermore, GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs coated GCE electrode demonstrated high selectivity owing to the presence of surface oxygen ions which in turn also enrich the active sites available for Hg2+ adsorption. Table 1 attached below illustrates the high efficiency of GCE-modified GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs towards Hg2+ detection compared to other previously reported literatures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and reproducibility of the GCE-modified with GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of the GCE-modified with GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs was assessed in the electrolyte solution comprising 50 μM Hg2+ in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0). For a total of 12 days, the electrochemical analysis was undergone once every 4 days, and the concentration of electrolytes was maintained similarly. It is noted that the oxidation current (Ipa = 7.26 × 10−4 A) remains same up to 4th day, subsequently, the oxidation peak current experiences a slight decrease on the 8th day (Ipa = 6.53 × 10−4 A) and 12th day (Ipa = 5.19 × 10−4 A). This slight decrease is owing to the deterioration of drop-casted GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs from the GCE surface (Fig. 11(a)). The hybrid NFs demonstrated an outstanding level of long-term stability, maintaining a substantial current retention rate of 71.5 %. Furthermore, the reproducibility of the GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs was studied by drop casting similar quantity of GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs on five different GCE after properly cleaning the surface of GCE and named as GCZ-1, GCZ-2, GCZ-3, GCZ-4, and GCZ-5. The Hg2+ current (7.26 × 10−4 A) resulted in a relative standard deviation of 1.81 % demonstrating the reproducibility of the sensor. According to these findings, the GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs modified electrode is stable, reproducible, and does not experience surface fouling (Fig. 11(b)). Most importantly, Hg2+ cannot be detected at the GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs modified glassy carbon electrode without accumulating time. Moreover, the ZnO and Ce-atom worked efficiently by offering a vast surface area necessary for electrochemical reaction and the surface oxygen vacancy that functioned as a charge trapper via enhancing the adsorption and oxidation of Hg2+ ions [128,129]. Hg2+ is effective towards the oxidation and adsorption of Ce/Zn rendering increased conductivity, sensitivity, and low detection limit. Finally, from the reproducibility and stability reports we strongly recommend that the synthesized GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs can selectively trace Hg (II) ions in water resources even in minor concentration (0.1 μM) without surface fouling and electrode retardation.

In summary, electrospun GQD functionalized Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs towards selective Hg2+ ion detection through DPV technique was demonstrated in this current research work. The GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs were synthesized through an optimized electrospinning procedure and utilized for the modification of GCE. The GCE electrode modified with GQD/Ce-ZnO hybrid NFs showcased prominent electrocatalytic behaviour against mercury oxidation (Hg2+). Remarkably, the integration of GQD into the Ce-ZnO NFs resulted in enhanced oxygen vacancy sites ( v
o), promoting the selective detection of Hg2+ ions even in the presence of interferents including cadmium, copper, bismuth, nickel, antimony, zinc. Consequently, the introduction of a higher concentration of oxygen vacancies ( v
o) within the synthesized nanofibers showcased a substantial increase in active sites, enabling excellent selectivity for Hg2+ adsorption and sufficient electrical conductivity. The utilization of UV-DRS and ESR analyses provides evidence supporting the presence of a higher concentration of oxygen vacancies ( v
o) in GQD/Ce-ZnO NFs compared to Ce-ZnO and GQD NFs. The constructed Hg (II) electrochemical sensor's lowest detection limit and linear range were determined to be 267 nM and 0.1 × 10−6 to 100 × 10−6 M. Furthermore, the GQD/Ce-ZnO NFs exhibited remarkable sensitivity of 1.67 μA μM−1 in detecting Hg (II) ions, showcasing their exceptional capability for Hg (II) ion detection. The GQD/Ce-ZnO NFs modified GCE demonstrated outstanding consistency, as evidenced by an impressively low RSD value of 1.81 %. Moreover, it exhibited remarkable long-term stability, with a significant current retention rate of 71.5 % over a span of 12 days, further highlighting its durability. Overall, the synthesized GQD/Ce-ZnO NFs offer the advantages of being easily fabricated, cost-effective, and thus highly recommended for the precise and selective detection of mercury ions.
